title: How Humanoid Robots Are Entering the Workforce

slides.heading: Humanoid Robots Are Clocking In
slides.body: From factory floors to warehouses, humanoid robots are no longer science fiction — they're showing up for work.

slides.heading: What Is a Humanoid Robot?
slides.body: Humanoid robots are machines built to mimic the human form — two legs, two arms, and a torso. Unlike specialized industrial arms, they're designed to navigate and operate in spaces built for people, using the same tools and environments we do.

slides.heading: Who's Building Them?
slides.body: Major players include Tesla (Optimus), Figure AI, Boston Dynamics (Atlas), Agility Robotics (Digit), and Chinese firms like Unitree. Investment has surged past $3 billion globally since 2024, reflecting intense confidence in the technology's near-term viability.

slides.heading: Where Are They Being Deployed?
slides.body: Early deployments focus on repetitive, physically demanding tasks: moving boxes in warehouses, assembling parts on auto production lines, and stocking shelves. BMW, Amazon, and GXO Logistics are among the first companies running pilots at scale.

slides.heading: The AI Brain Behind the Body
slides.body: Modern humanoid robots are driven by large neural networks trained on video and sensor data — similar to how LLMs learn language. They can generalize to new tasks without per-task programming, dramatically reducing deployment time compared to traditional industrial robots.

slides.heading: Why Humanoid Over Specialized Robots?
slides.body: Existing infrastructure — shelving heights, conveyor widths, tool handles — was designed for human bodies. A humanoid can slot into those workflows with minimal modification. Specialized robots often require expensive facility redesigns, making humanoids more cost-effective at scale.

slides.heading: How Safe Are They Around People?
slides.body: Safety is the top engineering challenge. Modern systems use force-limiting actuators, real-time collision detection, and redundant sensor arrays (LiDAR, cameras, tactile skin). Regulators in the US, EU, and South Korea are actively drafting co-worker safety standards.

slides.heading: What's the Cost Today?
slides.body: Current humanoid robots cost $30,000–$150,000 per unit — comparable to a mid-range industrial arm but with far greater versatility. Analysts expect prices to fall below $20,000 by 2028 as manufacturing scales, making them viable for small and mid-size businesses.

slides.heading: Jobs: Threat or Shift?
slides.body: Economists largely expect humanoid robots to automate the most dangerous and ergonomically harmful tasks first — roles with high injury rates and chronic labor shortages. Historically, automation creates new job categories (robot trainers, fleet operators, ethicists) even as it displaces others.

slides.heading: The Robot Colleague Is Real
slides.body: Humanoid robots are moving from labs to shop floors faster than most predicted. Understanding how they work — and where they're headed — is essential for anyone thinking about the future of work, technology, and society.
